Which of the following statements are true concerning the standardised residuals (residuals divided by their respective conditional standard deviations) from an estimated GARCH model?

i) They are assumed to be normally distributed


ii) Their squares will be related to their lagged squared values if the GARCH model is

appropriate


iii) In practice, they are likely to have fat tails


iv) If the GARCH model is adequate, the standardised residuals and the raw residuals

will be identical
(A) (i), (ii), and (iii) only
(B) (i), (ii), (iii), and (iv)
(C) (iii) and (iv) only
(D) (i) and (iv) only
(E) (i) and (iii) only
(F) (i), (iii), and (iv) only
(G) (ii) and (iv) only
(H) (i) and (ii) only
(I) (ii) and (iii) only
(J) (ii), (iii), and (iv) only
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
i) True. In the basic GARCH specification, the standardized innovations are assumed i.i.d. with mean zero and unit variance, commonly taken to be normal (unless a heavier-tailed distribution is explicitly specified).

ii) False. If the GARCH model is appropriate, the standardized residuals (and their squares) should exhibit no remaining autocorrelation or ARCH effects; squared standardized residuals should not be related to their lags.

iii) True. Empirically, even after standardization, innovations often display excess kurtosis unless a heavy-tailed distribution (e.g., Student-t) is modeled, so in practice fat tails are common.

iv) False. Standardization divides by the time-varying conditional standard deviation, so standardized and raw residuals will not be identical unless variance is constant.

Final answer: (E).

Which of the following choices is most likely to create the greatest decrease in real GDP?
(A) The government decreases both spending and taxes.
(B) The government holds spending and taxes constant.
(C) The government decreases spending matched with a decrease in taxes.
(D) The government decreases taxes with no change in spending.
(E) The government holds spending constant while increasing taxes.

(G) The government increases spending with a corresponding increase in taxes.
(H) The government increases taxes with no increase in spending.

target:
In the Keynesian AD framework, a change in government spending (G) shifts aggregate demand directly with the spending multiplier kG = 1/(1 − MPC), while a change in taxes (T) affects demand indirectly via consumption with a smaller tax multiplier kT = −MPC/(1 − MPC). Thus, per dollar, a cut in G reduces real GDP more than an equal increase in T. Among contractionary choices: (E) and (H) raise taxes with G constant—negative but smaller in magnitude than a G cut; (A) and (C) pair a G decrease with a tax decrease—offsetting effects, so the net drop is smaller than a pure G cut; (B) is neutral; (D), (F), (G), and (I) are expansionary. Therefore, a decrease in spending with no change in taxes (J) yields the greatest decrease in real GDP.
Final answer: (J).

Reductions = markdowns + shortages = 15,000 + 5,000 = 20,000.

In a secluded county, where prejudice festers and discrimination flourishes, there is a lovely lake, which the county has developed and maintained for recreational purposes. One of its biggest assets is the excellent fishing and boating, which is available to the public at large. The lease required the company to construct and operate a first-rate yacht house and club, complete with bar, restaurant, and private marina, and to pay the county 10% of its net profits as rent. The company set up bylaws, which were reviewed and approved by the county at the time the lease was negotiated. According to the bylaws, the yacht club, complete with its restaurant and bar, would be open to members only, and the membership committee was empowered to set up strict membership "standards," as well as the cost of membership fees and dues. Upon completion of the facilities, the state granted the company a license to sell alcoholic beverages in its restaurant and bar. The membership committee announced that the membership fee was $5,000 and the monthly dues $75. Furthermore, the membership committee had a policy of approving only membership applications from men, while disapproving and denying all applications from women. There were other similar facilities within the county available to women. A woman resident of the county brings suit against the company, claiming that her membership application was denied only because she is a woman, and that its policy of excluding women as a group denies her equal protection rights. Which of the following is the most accurate statement?

(B) The plaintiff will prevail if she can prove the company's policy is based on gender discrimination.
(C) The plaintiff will prevail, because the company's policy is discriminatory and violates her civil rights.
(D) The plaintiff will lose, because she can use other facilities within the county.
(E) The plaintiff will lose, because the company is a private entity and not subject to equal protection laws.
(F) The plaintiff will lose, because the company has the right to set its own membership criteria.
(G) The plaintiff will lose, because classifications based on sex have not yet been held to violate the equal protection clause.
(H) The plaintiff will prevail unless the company can prove some important basis for the exclusion of women.
(I) The plaintiff will lose, because other similar facilities are available to women.
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I).
target:
Let's think step by step. Equal Protection requires state action. Although the company is private, the county leased public parkland, approved the bylaws, and takes 10% of net profits—facts showing significant entwinement and a symbiotic relationship akin to Burton v. Wilmington Parking Authority. Thus, the club’s conduct is attributable to the state. Sex-based classifications are evaluated under intermediate scrutiny: the defendant must show an important governmental objective and a substantial relation to that objective (an “exceedingly persuasive justification”). The availability of other facilities is irrelevant, and mere preference or tradition won’t satisfy this standard. Therefore, the plaintiff will prevail unless the company can establish an important basis for excluding women.

Final answer: (H).

The animals having radial symmetry are considered to be at a lower evolutionary stage than those having bilateral symmetry. Why is this true? What is meant by the term secondarily radial?
(A) Secondarily radial refers to the primary development of radial symmetry in an organism
(B) Bilaterally symmetrical animals are considered more primitive due to their limited range of movement
(C) Radial symmetry provides better mobility

(I) Radially symmetrical animals have more complex reproductive systems
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I).
target:
Animals with radial symmetry (e.g., cnidarians) typically have simpler body organization, limited cephalization, diffuse nerve nets, and less specialized internal organs, suiting a sessile or drifting lifestyle. Bilateral symmetry correlates with directional movement, cephalization, centralized nervous systems, and greater organ specialization—features associated with more complex behavior and higher evolutionary grade. “Secondarily radial” describes lineages that evolved radial symmetry from bilaterally symmetrical ancestors; classic example: echinoderms, which are bilateral as larvae but become radial (often pentaradial) as adults.

Final answer: (E).

(B) the chef's improvements to the roadbed grant him additional rights to the easement.
(C) the chef and the assistant, being in privity of estate, could "tack on" their successive use periods.
(D) an easement appurtenant is alienable in that any conveyance transferring possession of the dominant tenement also passes the easement privilege.
(E) the assistant, as a bonafide purchaser, is protected from the neighbor's obstruction, even though the easement was unrecorded.
(F) because the easement was created by implication, the chef's prior and continuous use gave rise to a prescriptive easement.

target:

The neighbor granted, in writing, a right-of-way over his land for access to the chef’s lakefront property—an express easement appurtenant benefiting the dominant estate (the cottage property). An appurtenant easement runs with the land and automatically passes with any conveyance of the dominant tenement, even without specific mention. Thus, when the chef sold the cottage to his assistant, the easement transferred as well, giving the assistant the same right to use the roadway and to demand removal of obstructions. Other options miss the mark: (A) “misuse” concerns the dominant holder’s overuse, not the servient owner’s business; (B) improvements don’t enlarge easement rights; (C) tacking is for prescriptive rights, not needed for an express grant; (E) BFP doctrine protects against unrecorded burdens, not to assert one’s own easement; (F) the easement was not by implication/prescription; (G) improvement rights are not the core issue; (H) damages aren’t the primary remedy here; (I) long-term use to claim ownership is inapposite. Final answer: (D).

In a separate experiment in the same calorimeter, the combustion of $0.825 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzoic acid, for which the internal energy of combustion is $-3251 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, gave a temperature rise of $1.940 \mathrm{~K}$. Calculate the enthalpy of formation of D-ribose.
(A) -1210 $\mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(B) -1130 $\mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(C) -1285 $\mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(D) -1270 $\mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(E) -1150 $\mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(F) -1450 $\mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(G) -1200 $\mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(H) -1500 $\mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(I) -1340 $\mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(J) -1380 $\mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Calibrate the calorimeter with benzoic acid:
- n(benzoic) = 0.825 g / 122.12 g mol^-1 ≈ 0.00676 mol
- q_cal = −ΔU = 0.00676 mol × 3251 kJ mol^-1 ≈ 21.98 kJ
- C_cal = q_cal/ΔT = 21.98 kJ / 1.940 K ≈ 11.32 kJ K^-1

For ribose:
- q_cal = C_cal × ΔT = 11.32 kJ K^-1 × 0.910 K ≈ 10.31 kJ
- n(ribose) = 0.727 g / 150.13 g mol^-1 ≈ 0.00484 mol
- At constant volume, ΔU_comb (per mol) = −q_cal / n ≈ −10.31 kJ / 0.00484 mol ≈ −2.13 × 10^3 kJ mol^-1

Convert to ΔH_comb: for C5H10O5(s) + 5 O2(g) → 5 CO2(g) + 5 H2O(l), Δn_gas = 5 − 5 = 0, so ΔH_comb ≈ ΔU_comb ≈ −2130 kJ mol^-1.

Use formation enthalpies: ΔHf°[CO2(g)] ≈ −393.5 kJ mol^-1, ΔHf°[H2O(l)] ≈ −285.8 kJ mol^-1.
Sum(products) = 5(−393.5) + 5(−285.8) ≈ −3396.5 kJ mol^-1.
ΔHf°(ribose) = Sum(products) − ΔH_comb ≈ −3396.5 − (−2130) ≈ −1.27 × 10^3 kJ mol^-1.

Final answer: (D).

Neural signaling’s “electrical” phase is the action potential propagating along the neuron, which depends critically on insulation and rapid conduction. A father and a son were indicted and charged with bank robbery. They allegedly participated together in robbing a bank in their hometown. After the son was arrested and given Miranda warnings, he confessed and admitted that he and his father took part in the bank robbery. The prosecution decided to charge the father and son in the same indictment and prosecute them together in a single trial. At trial, the son took the stand and recanted his earlier confession by denying participation in the robbery. The prosecution now seeks to introduce into evidence the son's earlier confession that was videotaped by the police. The son and the father both object to its admissibility. The confession should be
(A) admitted against the father only, because the son recanted his confession.
(B) not admitted against the son, but admitted against the father as it implicates him in the crime.
(C) admitted against both the son and the father, because the son implicated the father in the crime.
(D) not admitted against the son or the father, because the son was under duress when he confessed.
(E) admitted against the son only, because it is his confession.
(F) admitted against both the son and the father, because they were jointly charged.
(G) not admitted against the son or the father, because the confession is hearsay not within any recognized exception.
(H) not admitted against the son or the father, because the son recanted the confession.
(I) admitted against both the son and the father, because they were engaged in a conspiracy.
(J) admitted against the son only.
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Let’s think step by step. The son’s videotaped confession is an out-of-court statement. As to the son, it is admissible as a statement of a party-opponent (an admission), and his later recantation does not bar its use; at minimum it’s also a prior inconsistent statement usable for impeachment. As to the father, because the prosecution is proceeding on a joint participation theory (a conspiracy/joint venture), a participant’s statement that implicates a co-participant is admissible against the other if made during and in furtherance of the conspiracy; such co-conspirator statements are treated as nonhearsay. In addition, any Confrontation Clause concern is alleviated because the son took the stand and was subject to cross-examination, so there is no Bruton bar. It is not admissible merely because they are jointly charged (rejecting F), nor excluded simply because the son recanted (rejecting H) or on hearsay grounds (rejecting G). Thus it may be used against both.

Final answer: (I).

A couple comes for preconceptional genetic counseling because they both have a family history of α-thalassemia. The woman has a minimally decreased hemoglobin concentration. Genetic studies show a single gene deletion. The man has microcytic anemia and a two-gene deletion. If the two-gene deletion is in trans (one deletion on the maternal gene and one deletion on the paternal gene), which of the following percentages of their offspring will have a two-gene deletion?
(A) 80%
(B) 100%
(C) 20%
(D) 0%
(E) 66%
(F) 50%
(G) 33%
(H) 25%
(I) 75%
(J) 10%
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Each person has four α-globin genes (αα/αα). The woman with a single-gene deletion is -α/αα. The man with a two-gene deletion in trans is -α/-α. His gametes are all -α. Her gametes are 50% -α and 50% αα. Thus, half of the offspring (-α from father × -α from mother) will be -α/-α (two-gene deletion), and half (-α × αα) will be -α/αα (single-gene deletion). Therefore, 50% will have a two-gene deletion.

Final answer: (F).

A spherical automobile component of 6 in diameter, ini-tially at 80°F, is placed in a cubical oven of side 5 ft which is at a temperature of 560°F. Considering both of them as black bodies, calculate the net heat transferred from the oven to the component.
(A) 1750 Btu/hr
(B) 1500 Btu/hr
(C) 2000 Btu/hr
(D) 1000 Btu/hr
(E) 1450 Btu/hr
(F) 2200 Btu/hr
(G) 1250 Btu/hr
(H) 1344 Btu/hr
(I) 1800 Btu/hr
(J) 1600 Btu/hr
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
A: For a small black body inside a large black isothermal enclosure, the net radiative heat gain is q = σ A (T_env^4 − T_s^4). Convert temperatures to Rankine: T_env = 560 + 460 = 1020 R, T_s = 80 + 460 = 540 R. Sphere diameter d = 6 in = 0.5 ft, so area A = π d^2 = π(0.5)^2 = 0.7854 ft^2. Use σ = 0.1714×10^−8 Btu/(hr·ft^2·R^4). Compute T_env^4 − T_s^4 via (a^4 − b^4) = (a^2 − b^2)(a^2 + b^2): 1020^2 = 1,040,400; 540^2 = 291,600; difference = 748,800; sum = 1,332,000; product = 9.974016×10^11 R^4. Then heat flux σΔT^4 ≈ 0.1714×10^−8 × 9.974016×10^11 ≈ 1,709.6 Btu/(hr·ft^2). Multiply by area: q ≈ 1,709.6 × 0.7854 ≈ 1,343 Btu/hr ≈ 1,344 Btu/hr.

Final answer: (H).

The positive integers N and N^2 both end in the same sequence of four digits abcd when written in base 10, where digit a is nonzero. Find the three-digit number abc.

(C) 625

target:
We need N ≡ N^2 (mod 10000), so N(N−1) ≡ 0 (mod 10000). Since 10000 = 16·625, we must have N ≡ 0 or 1 (mod 16) and N ≡ 0 or 1 (mod 625). The trivial classes N ≡ 0 or 1 (mod 10000) give last four digits 0000 or 0001, which are invalid because a ≠ 0. So consider the mixed cases. Solving r ≡ 0 (mod 16) and r ≡ 1 (mod 625) gives r = 9376. A wire of diameter 1 mm carrying a heavy current has a temperature of 1200°K when equilibrium has been achieved. We assume that the heat lost from the wire is purely by radiation. The temperature of the sur-roundings is 300°K, the resistivity of the wire at this temperature is 5 × 10^-8 \Omega \bullet m, and the temperature coefficient of resistance of the wire is 4 × 10^-3 per C deg. What is the magnitude of the current in the wire?
(A) 35.5A
(B) 28.5A
(C) 25.0A
(D) 40.0A
(E) 50.0A
(F) 55.0A
(G) 30.0A
(H) 60.0A
(I) 20.0A
(J) 45.0A
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
A wire at steady state loses electrical heating I^2R per unit length by blackbody radiation from its surface. For diameter d = 1 mm, radius r = 5×10^-4 m:
- Surface area per unit length: A_s/L = πd = π×10^-3 ≈ 3.1416×10^-3 m^2/m.
- Cross-sectional area: A = πr^2 = π(5×10^-4)^2 ≈ 7.854×10^-7 m^2.

Resistivity at 300 K is ρ_300 = 5×10^-8 Ω·m with temperature coefficient α = 4×10^-3/°C. At 1200 K, ΔT = 900 K, so
ρ_1200 = ρ_300(1 + αΔT) = 5×10^-8(1 + 0.004×900) = 5×10^-8×4.6 = 2.3×10^-7 Ω·m.
Resistance per unit length: R' = ρ/A = (2.3×10^-7)/(7.854×10^-7) ≈ 0.293 Ω/m.

Radiative loss per unit length (ε ≈ 1): 
P'_rad = σ(A_s/L)(T^4 − T_0^4) with σ = 5.67×10^-8, T = 1200 K, T_0 = 300 K.
T^4 − T_0^4 = 1200^4 − 300^4 = 2.0736×10^12 − 8.1×10^9 ≈ 2.0655×10^12.
Thus σ(T^4 − T_0^4) ≈ 5.67×10^-8 × 2.0655×10^12 ≈ 1.1718×10^5 W/m^2.
So P'_rad ≈ 1.1718×10^5 × 3.1416×10^-3 ≈ 368 W/m.

Equate electrical heating and radiation per meter: I^2 R' = P'_rad ⇒ I^2 = 368/0.293 ≈ 1256 ⇒ I ≈ 35.5 A.

Final answer: (A).

There are two highly inbred strains of laboratory mice whose adult body weights are very different. Assume that the mouse's body weight is under the control of three pairs of contrasting genes: A vs. a, B vs. b and D vs. d. Assume further that each capital letter gene is responsible for contributing 5.0 grams to the total body weight, and that lowercase letters contribute 2.5 grams to total body weight. The average weight of mice in Strain I is 30 grams, while that of Strain II mice is 15 grams. (a) What are the most likely genotypes of these two strains? (b) Suppose Strain I and Strain II are crossed. What will be the phenotype of the F_1 progeny?
(A) Strain I: AABB DD, Strain II: aabb dd, F_1 progeny: 20g
(B) Strain I: AAbb DD, Strain II: aaBB dd, F_1 progeny: 25g
(C) Strain I: AA BB DD, Strain II: aabb dd, F_1 progeny: 22.5g
(D) Strain I: AABB, Strain II: aabb, F_1 progeny: 20g
(E) Strain I: AABB Dd, Strain II: aabb DD, F_1 progeny: 21.25g
(F) Strain I: AA BB DD, Strain II: aa BB dd, F_1 progeny: 30g
(G) Strain I: AA BB DD, Strain II: aa bb DD, F_1 progeny: 27.5g
(H) Strain I: Aa Bb Dd, Strain II: Aa Bb Dd, F_1 progeny: 30g
(I) Strain I: AA bb dd, Strain II: aa BB DD, F_1 progeny: 22.5g
(J) Strain I: AA bb DD, Strain II: aabb dd, F_1 progeny: 25g
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
A: Let's think step by step. Highly inbred strains are homozygous at all loci. Each uppercase allele contributes 5.0 g and each lowercase allele 2.5 g; with three loci there are six alleles total.

- Strain I weighs 30 g, which equals 6 × 5.0 g, so it must be AA BB DD.
- Strain II weighs 15 g, which equals 6 × 2.5 g, so it must be aabbdd.

Crossing AA BB DD × aabbdd gives F1: Aa Bb Dd. Each heterozygous locus contributes 5.0 + 2.5 = 7.5 g; across three loci the F1 weight is 3 × 7.5 = 22.5 g.

Thus the correct choice is (C).

Final answer: (C).

Use partied fraction expansion to find the inverse z-transform of F (z) = [(30z^2 - 12 z) / (6z^2 - 5z + 1)].
(A) f(n) = [3 (1/2)^n + 1 (1/3)^n + 1] u[n]
(B) f(n) = [4 (1/2)^n - 1 (1/3)^n] u[n]
(C) f(n) = [3 (1/2)^n - 2 (1/3)^n] u[n-1]
(D) f (n) = [3 (1/2)^n + 2 (1/3)^n] n[n]
(E) f (n) = [2 (1/2)^n + 3 (1/3)^n] u[n]
(F) f(n) = [5 (1/2)^n + 3 (1/3)^n] u[n]
(G) f(n) = [3 (1/2)^n + 2 (1/3)^n] u[n-1]
(H) f (n) = [3 (1/2)^n + 2 (1/3)^n] u[n]
(I) f (n) = [3 (1/2)^n - 2 (1/3)^n] u[n]
(J) f(n) = [2 (1/2)^n + 3 (1/3)^n] n[n-1]
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Factor the denominator: 6z^2 − 5z + 1 = (3z − 1)(2z − 1) = 6(z − 1/3)(z − 1/2). Then
F(z) = (30z^2 − 12z)/(6z^2 − 5z + 1) = (5z^2 − 2z)/[(z − 1/3)(z − 1/2)].
Use the causal form z/(z − a): write F(z) = A z/(z − 1/3) + B z/(z − 1/2).
Equate numerators: 5z^2 − 2z = A z(z − 1/2) + B z(z − 1/3) = z[(A + B)z − (A/2 + B/3)].
Thus A + B = 5 and A/2 + B/3 = 2. Solving gives A = 2, B = 3.
Hence f[n] = 2(1/3)^n u[n] + 3(1/2)^n u[n] = [3(1/2)^n + 2(1/3)^n] u[n].

Final answer: (H).

(B) easement appurtenant.

(A) V = 3.2 ft^3, P_t = 128psia
(B) V = 2.766 ft^3, P_t = 124.92psia
(C) V = 2.5 ft^3, P_t = 120psia
(D) V = 2.2 ft^3, P_t = 140psia
(E) V = 3.0 ft^3, P_t = 115psia

target:
Since the water vapor is saturated at 250°F, its partial pressure equals the saturation pressure and its specific volume equals v_g at 250°F. From steam tables: at 250°F, v_g ≈ 13.83 ft^3/lbm and P_sat ≈ 29.9 psia. Therefore, the tank volume is set by the vapor: V = m_v v_g = 0.2 lbm × 13.83 ft^3/lbm ≈ 2.766 ft^3. The air behaves ideally: with R_air = 0.3704 ft^3·psia/(lbm·R) and T = 709.67 R, P_air = m R T / V = (1)(0.3704)(709.67)/2.766 ≈ 94.93 psia. Total pressure is the sum of partial pressures: P_t = P_air + P_v ≈ 94.93 + 29.99 ≈ 124.92 psia.

Final answer: (B).

A: Let’s think step by step. The defendant unequivocally invoked his Sixth Amendment right to self-representation. Under Faretta v. California, once a competent defendant clearly and timely elects to proceed pro se and makes a knowing, intelligent waiver of counsel, the court cannot deny that right merely because the judge finds the defendant’s reasons “unpersuasive” or because he lacks legal experience. The colloquy reflects awareness and a clear choice; forcing counsel on him violated Faretta and requires reversal of the conviction.

As to jury trial, there is no constitutional right to a bench trial; the prosecution and court may insist on a jury (Singer v. United States), and the offense here (maximum seven months) triggers the jury right. So impaneling a jury over the defendant’s waiver was not error.

The reimbursement order must also be reversed. The state cannot recoup fees for counsel improperly imposed on a defendant who had the right to represent himself; moreover, recoupment generally requires safeguards including an ability-to-pay determination, which is absent here for an indigent defendant.

Therefore, both the conviction and the reimbursement order should be reversed because the defendant was denied the right to represent himself.

Final answer: (G).

In 1996, a developer purchased a 100-acre tract located in a northern county in a state. Shortly thereafter, the developer prepared a subdivision plan that created 100 one-acre residential building lots on this tract. In 1997, the subdivision plan was recorded with the county recorder's office. During the next few years, the developer sold 60 residential lots to individual purchasers. Each deed specified that every lot designated on the subdivision plan was to be recorded in the county recorder's office. Each deed also provided the following:"No house trailer or mobile home shall be built or maintained on any lot within the subdivision. "In 2003, the developer conveyed the remaining 40 lots to a builder by deed that included language identical to that contained in the first 60 deeds. This deed from the developer to the builder was recorded. By 2008, the builder had sold all of the 40 lots. Each of these deeds identified each lot as being a part of the subdivision, but did not include the clause relating to mobile homes. On January 30, 2009, a buyer, who had purchased one of the residential lots from the builder, placed a mobile home on his property. Which of the following statements is LEAST accurate with respect to the buyer's deed?
(A) The covenant prohibiting mobile homes ran with the land as far as the builder, but not as far as the buyer.
(B) The covenant prohibiting mobile homes could be enforced by any subdivision lot owner.
(C) The buyer should have had constructive notice of the restriction against mobile homes.
(D) All subsequent grantees of the builder would be in privity of estate.
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D).
target:
A: Let's think step by step. The developer created a uniform restriction against mobile homes in a recorded subdivision, placing the clause in the first 60 deeds and in the recorded deed conveying the remaining 40 lots to the builder. That establishes a common scheme and intent for the restriction to run to all lots, and the restriction plainly touches and concerns the land.

- Constructive notice: The buyer’s chain of title includes the recorded developer-to-builder deed containing the restriction, and the recorded subdivision plan and pattern of earlier deeds provide at least inquiry/record notice. Thus (C) is accurate.

- Enforceability: Under the common scheme/reciprocal negative servitude doctrine, any lot owner in the subdivision may enforce the restriction against other lot owners, even if a later deed omits the clause. Thus (B) is accurate.

- Privity: The covenant was in a deed between developer and builder (horizontal privity), and the buyer took the full estate from the builder (vertical privity), so subsequent grantees of the builder are in privity of estate. Thus (D) is accurate.

Given intent, touch and concern, privity, and notice, the burden runs to the buyer both as a real covenant and at least as an equitable servitude. Therefore the statement that it ran only as far as the builder but not to the buyer is the least accurate. 

Final answer: (A).

2 moles of a monoatomic ideal gas at 45°C are compressed adiabatically and reversibly from 75 liters to 20 liters. Calculate q, W, \DeltaH and \DeltaE for the process, excluding vi-brationalcontributions.
(A) q=0, ΔE=15.00kJ, ΔH=22.50kJ
(B) q=11.21kJ, ΔE=0, ΔH=18.68kJ
(C) q=0, ΔE=18.68kJ, ΔH=11.21kJ
(D) q=18.68kJ, ΔE=18.68kJ, ΔH=18.68kJ
(E) q=0, ΔE=11.21kJ, ΔH=18.68kJ
(F) q=11.21kJ, ΔE=18.68kJ, ΔH=0
(G) q=22.50kJ, ΔE=15.00kJ, ΔH=0
(H) q=0, ΔE=22.50kJ, ΔH=15.00kJ

target:
For a reversible adiabatic (q = 0) compression of a monoatomic ideal gas, γ = Cp/Cv = (5/2)/(3/2) = 5/3. The adiabatic relation gives T2 = T1 (V1/V2)^{γ−1} = 318 K × (75/20)^{2/3} ≈ 318 × 2.414 ≈ 768 K, so ΔT ≈ 450 K. For a monoatomic ideal gas, Cv = (3/2)R and Cp = (5/2)R, so:
- ΔE = n Cv ΔT = 2 × (3/2)R × 450 ≈ 3R × 450 ≈ 11.21 kJ
- ΔH = n Cp ΔT = 2 × (5/2)R × 450 ≈ 5R × 450 ≈ 18.68 kJ
With q = 0 (adiabatic), this matches q = 0, ΔE ≈ 11.21 kJ, ΔH ≈ 18.68 kJ.

Final answer: (E).

The purpose of "augmenting" the Dickey-Fuller test regression is to
(A) Ensure that there is no heteroscedasticity in the test regression residuals.
(B) Improve the accuracy of the Dickey-Fuller test
(C) Ensure that the test regression residuals are negatively skewed
(D) Ensure that the test regression residuals are normally distributed
(E) Ensure that all of the non-stationarity is taken into account.
(F) Ensure that the test regression residuals have no outliers
(G) Ensure that the test regression residuals are positively skewed
(H) Ensure that there is no autocorrelation in the test regression residuals
(I) Ensure that the test regression residuals are balanced
(J) Increase the complexity of the Dickey-Fuller test
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
The plain Dickey-Fuller test assumes white-noise errors; if the data have higher-order dynamics, the residuals can be autocorrelated and the DF test statistics become invalid. The augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) regression includes lagged differences of the series precisely to absorb higher-order serial correlation so that the residuals are approximately white noise. It is not primarily aimed at fixing heteroscedasticity, normality, skewness, outliers, or “taking into account all non-stationarity”; increased complexity is a byproduct, not the goal. Therefore, the purpose is to eliminate autocorrelation in the test residuals.

Final answer: (H).

The work done by the retarding force equals the loss of kinetic energy: F d2 = (1/2) m v^2 − (1/2)(M + m)u^2 = (1/2) m v^2 [1 − m/(M + m)] = (1/2) m v^2 · M/(M + m). Let d be the well’s depth (ft). Total time = fall time + sound travel time = sqrt(2d/32) + d/1100 = sqrt(d/16) + d/1100 = 2. Let x = sqrt(d). Then x/4 + x^2/1100 = 2 ⇒ multiply by 2200: 2x^2 + 550x − 4400 = 0 ⇒ x^2 + 275x − 2200 = 0. The positive root is x = [−275 + √(275^2 + 4·2200)]/2 = [−275 + √84425]/2 ≈ 7.78, so d = x^2 ≈ 60.6 ft, matching (J).

What is a derogation?
(A) A State may suspend particular rights by means of a derogation in circumstances that threaten its existence
(B) A derogation is a process whereby a state can amend a treaty

target:
A derogation in human rights law is a temporary suspension of certain treaty obligations in a public emergency threatening the life of the nation (e.g., ICCPR art. 4; ECHR art. 15). It is limited to measures strictly required by the exigencies, must be non-discriminatory, notified, and cannot touch non-derogable rights. (C), (F) misdescribe them as enforcement/penalty clauses. (D) is wrong because derogation applies in emergencies, not times of peace. (E) is about standing or right of petition. (G) states a general duty, not derogation. (I) describes breach, not a lawful mechanism. (J) is overbroad and missing the emergency and strict-necessity constraints.

Final answer: (A).

(B) The TOT state is exclusively linked to the recall of proper nouns.
(C) Age is an irrelevant factor in the frequency of TOT occurrences.

target:
Studies of the tip-of-the-tongue (TOT) phenomenon show that people often have access to both semantic information (meaning, related concepts, category) and phonological/phonetic information (first letter, syllable count, stress pattern, partial sounds) about the target word, and these cues can help trigger retrieval. Hence, people use both semantic and phonetic characteristics during retrieval.

Final answer: (D).

A researcher notices that the frequency of a note emitted by an automobile horn appears to drop from 284 cycles \bullet s^-1 to 266 cycles \bullet s^-1 as the automobile passes him. From this observation he is able to calculate the speed of the car, knowing that the speed of sound in air is 1100 ft \bullet s^-1. What value does he obtain for the speed?
(A) 30 mph
(B) 20 mph
(C) 32 mph
(D) 28.5 mph
(E) 26 mph
(F) 22 mph
(G) 18 mph
(H) 17 mph
(I) 24.5 mph
(J) 35 mph
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
As the car approaches, the observed frequency is higher (284 Hz), and as it recedes, it is lower (266 Hz). For a moving source and stationary observer:
- f_approach = f · v/(v − vs)
- f_recede = f · v/(v + vs)

Taking the ratio: (f_approach)/(f_recede) = (v + vs)/(v − vs) = 284/266 = 142/133.
Solve for vs/v: (r − 1)/(r + 1) = [(142/133) − 1]/[(142/133) + 1] = (9/133)/(275/133) = 9/275.
Thus vs = v · 9/275 = 1100 · 9/275 = 36 ft/s.

Convert to mph: 36 ft/s ÷ 1.4667 ≈ 24.5 mph.

Final answer: (I).

target:
For most of U.S. history—from Washington’s Farewell Address through the 19th century and up to World War II—the prevailing approach was to avoid entangling alliances and steer clear of European power politics. A company exchanged land with an appraised value of $50,000 and an original cost of $20,000 for machinery with a fair value of $55,000. Assuming that the transaction has commercial substance, what is the gain on the exchange?
(A) $45,000
(B) $5,000
(C) $30,000
(D) $25,000
(E) $0
(F) $20,000
(G) $15,000
(H) $35,000
(I) $50,000
(J) $10,000
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Let's think step by step. Because the exchange has commercial substance, the gain is recognized in full and is measured as the fair value of the asset given up minus its carrying amount. The land’s carrying amount is $20,000 and its fair (appraised) value is $50,000, so the gain is $50,000 − $20,000 = $30,000. The stated $55,000 fair value of the machinery is not used to measure the gain when the fair value of the asset surrendered is available.

Final answer: (C).

An auto mechanic was repairing a car in his auto. shop when a spark ignited gasoline and oil residue on the floor. The mechanic managed to douse the flames, but not before the fire created giant billows of smoke that floated over onto a neighbor's property and caused the neighbor's home to become discolored. If the neighbor asserts a claim against the mechanic, the neighbor would most likely be able to recover for
(A) conversion.
(B) vicarious liability.
(C) defamation.
(D) intentional infliction of emotional distress.
(E) nuisance.
(F) strict liability.
(G) negligence.
(H) assault and battery.
(I) trespass.
(J) invasion of privacy.
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Let's think step by step. The neighbor suffered property damage (discoloration) from smoke produced by a fire negligently allowed to start in the mechanic’s shop. The mechanic owed a duty of reasonable care in handling flammable substances; allowing a spark to ignite gasoline/oil residue on the floor can be a breach, and the resulting smoke damage is a foreseeable harm, satisfying causation and damages—classic negligence.

Other torts do not fit: conversion concerns chattels, not land (A); vicarious liability is a derivative doctrine, not a standalone claim (B); defamation and invasion of privacy (C, J) are inapplicable; IIED requires extreme/outrageous conduct (D), not present; assault and battery (H) require offensive/physical contact or apprehension of such, not present; strict liability (F) doesn’t apply because auto repair is not an abnormally dangerous activity; trespass (I) generally requires an intentional physical invasion—accidental smoke from a negligently started fire is not intentional. While nuisance (E) addresses substantial and unreasonable interference with use/enjoyment (often by ongoing or intentional conduct), a single accidental episode causing property damage is most straightforwardly remedied in negligence.

Final answer: (G).

The police were called to a domestic dispute. The wife told police that her husband was abusive and that he was a heavy cocaine user. The husband was present and denied the allegation. Police asked for permission to search the premises for drugs, which was consented to by the wife. The husband, however, refused to consent to the search. The police went ahead anyway and found drugs in the husband's belongings. After being arrested on drug charges, the husband filed a motion for suppression of the evidence based on lack of consent. What should be the likely decision of the trial court?
(A) The search was valid because the husband's denial of the allegations was not credible.
(B) The search was invalid because the wife was not authorized to give consent to search her husband's personal belongings.
(C) The search was invalid because the police did not have a search warrant, which is required regardless of consent.
(D) The search was invalid and the evidence must be suppressed because the co-occupant of the premises was present and specifically refused to give consent to the search.
(E) The search was valid because the cases say that one occupant's consent to the search overrules the other occupant's refusal to consent.
(F) The search was valid because the wife's statement established probable cause that a crime was being committed  this created exigent circumstances for the search.
(G) The search was valid because the wife's consent was sufficient to search the common areas of the home.
(H) The search was invalid because the consent to search was not voluntary, as it was given out of fear of domestic violence.
(I) The search was valid because the drugs were in plain view when the police entered the premises.
(J) The search was invalid because, even though consent was given, the police must first attempt to get a search warrant to enter a home.
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Under Georgia v. Randolph, when a physically present co-occupant expressly refuses consent, the police may not conduct a warrantless search based on the other co-occupant’s consent. Here, the husband was present and specifically refused; thus the wife’s consent could not authorize the search, including of common areas, and certainly not of his personal belongings. The evidence must be suppressed.

Final answer: (D).

Including relevant lagged values of the dependent variable on the right hand side of a regression equation could lead to which one of the following?
(A) Biased, inconsistent and inefficient coefficient estimates
(B) Unbiased and inconsistent but efficient coefficient estimates
(C) Biased and inconsistent coefficient estimates
(D) Biased but inconsistent but efficient coefficient estimates
(E) Unbiased, consistent and efficient coefficient estimates
(F) Unbiased and consistent but efficient coefficient estimates
(G) Unbiased and consistent but inefficient coefficient estimates.
(H) Unbiased but inconsistent coefficient estimates
(I) Biased and consistent but inefficient coefficient estimates

target:
A: Let’s think step by step. Putting lagged dependent variables on the RHS (e.g., y_{t-1}) makes the regressor contain past shocks, creating finite-sample endogeneity with the current error (Hurwicz/Nickell-type bias). Hence OLS is biased in finite samples. However, under standard time-series assumptions (no contemporaneous correlation of u_t with y_{t-1} and large T), this bias shrinks to zero, so the coefficient estimates are consistent. Therefore, they are biased but consistent.

Final answer: (J).

Which of the following is not a non-coding RNA?
(A) lncRNA
(B) piRNA
(C) rRNA
(D) snRNA
(E) tRNA
(F) miRNA
(G) siRNA
(H) Ribozyme
(I) mRNA
(J) gRNA
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Non-coding RNAs are functional RNAs that do not serve as templates for protein synthesis. lncRNA, piRNA, rRNA, snRNA, tRNA, miRNA, siRNA, ribozymes, and gRNA are all non-coding functional RNAs involved in regulation, catalysis, translation machinery, splicing, or editing. mRNA, by contrast, is the coding RNA that carries the template for translation into protein. Hence, the one that is not a non-coding RNA is mRNA.

Final answer: (I).

In a healthy person maintaining energy balance in equilibrium (i.e. mathematical equality of energy intake and energy expenditure) is generally achieved on a:
(A) 4-5 day basis
(B) 3-4 day basis
(C) 5-6 day basis
(D) Hourly basis
(E) Monthly basis
(F) 1-2 week basis
(G) Weekly basis
(H) 1-2 month basis
(I) 2-3 day basis
(J) day-to-day basis
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
A: Let's think step by step. Energy intake and expenditure in healthy people fluctuate substantially from day to day due to variable food intake, activity, and substrate storage/oxidation, so exact balance is not achieved hourly or day-to-day. Short windows (2–6 days) are still too noisy for full compensation. Conversely, if balance were only achieved monthly or longer, weight would drift more than typically observed. Physiologic appetite/energy-expenditure compensation and buffering by glycogen and fat stores tend to bring average intake and expenditure into alignment over about 1–2 weeks, which best fits observed stability in body weight and doubly labeled water measurements. Hence 1–2 weeks is the most accurate timescale.

Final answer: (F).

The cells of humans and most animals have become adapted to surviving only within a relatively small range of hydrogen ion concentrations. What is the role of the kidneys in regulating hydrogen ion concentration?
(A) The kidneys filter out hydrogen ions and recycle them into other cellular processes.
(B) The kidneys regulate the hydrogen ion concentration of the extracellular fluids by excreting either acidic or basic constituents when the levels deviate from normal.
(C) The kidneys maintain a constant production of hydrogen ions, regardless of the body's needs.
(D) The kidneys produce hydrogen ions to maintain balance.
(E) The kidneys eliminate all hydrogen ions from the body.
(F) The kidneys detect hydrogen ion levels and signal other organs to adjust their pH levels accordingly.
(G) The kidneys release enzymes that directly neutralize hydrogen ions in the bloodstream.
(H) The kidneys convert hydrogen ions into inert gases that are exhaled.
(I) The kidneys store hydrogen ions for future use.
(J) The kidneys absorb all hydrogen ions, preventing them from affecting the body's pH balance.
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
A: Let's think step by step. Long-term regulation of body pH is a renal function: the kidneys adjust extracellular H+ by varying tubular H+ secretion and bicarbonate reabsorption/creation. In acidosis they excrete more acid (H+, titratable acid, NH4+) and regenerate HCO3−, producing acidic urine; in alkalosis they excrete base (bicarbonate), producing alkaline urine. They do not eliminate all H+, store it, or convert it to gases; nor do they merely signal other organs. Thus the kidneys regulate ECF hydrogen ion concentration by excreting acidic or basic constituents as needed.

Final answer: (B).

Find the value of $y_0$ for which the solution of the initial value problem
$$
y^{\prime}-y=1+3 \sin t, \quad y(0)=y_0
$$
remains finite as $t \rightarrow \infty$
(A) -3.5
(B) 1.5
(C) -1
(D) -1.5
(E) 0
(F) -2.5
(G) 0.5
(H) -4.5
(I) 2.5
(J) 3.5
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
A: Let's think step by step. Solve y' − y = 1 + 3 sin t. Homogeneous solution: y_h = C e^t. For a particular solution: for 1, take k constant: 0 − k = 1 ⇒ k = −1. For 3 sin t, try A sin t + B cos t: y' − y gives cos coefficient A − B = 0 ⇒ A = B, and sin coefficient −(A + B) = 3 ⇒ A = B = −3/2. So y_p = −1 − (3/2)(sin t + cos t). Thus y(t) = C e^t − 1 − (3/2)(sin t + cos t). To remain finite as t → ∞, need C = 0. From y(0) = y0 = C − 1 − 3/2 = C − 5/2, we get C = y0 + 5/2, so C = 0 implies y0 = −5/2 = −2.5.

Final answer: (F).

A 22-year-old male presents to the office with a 5-day history of diarrhea after completing his third course of antibiotics for mastoiditis. Physical examination reveals vague generalized abdominal pain on palpation. Culture on hektoen enteric agar is positive. The most likely etiologic agent causing the diarrhea is
(A) Clostridium difficile
(B) Entamoeba histolytica
(C) Vibrio cholerae
(D) Escherichia coli
(E) Staphylococcus aureus
(F) Campylobacter jejuni
(G) Giardia lamblia
(H) Salmonella typhi
(I) Yersinia enterocolitica
(J) Shigella dysenteriae
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Let's think step by step. Post-antibiotic diarrhea classically suggests Clostridium difficile, but C. difficile (a gram-positive anaerobe) is not recovered on Hektoen enteric agar. Hektoen enteric agar is selective/differential for enteric gram-negatives, especially Salmonella and Shigella; Salmonella produces H2S (black-centered) colonies, whereas Shigella does not. Among the choices, the pathogens that fit HE agar recovery are Salmonella and Shigella. The clinical picture lacks prominent dysentery (blood, tenesmus) typical of Shigella dysenteriae, making Salmonella more likely. Other listed organisms are identified on different media or are not bacterial enterics (e.g., Vibrio on TCBS, Campylobacter on Campy agar, S. aureus preformed toxin with rapid-onset vomiting, protozoa not grown on HE). Therefore, the best match is Salmonella typhi.

Final answer: (H).

(A) Lack of available organs
(B) Graft rejection due to immunological reaction

A retiree owned a 100-acre farm. For many years, the retiree grew tobacco on a 10-acre strip located in the northeast section of the property. In March, the retiree planted his annual tobacco crop, which he usually harvested in early October. In September, the retiree sold his farm to a tobacco grower for $100,000. At the time the retiree conveyed the property to the grower, the tobacco crop was well developed and quite mature. When the retiree and the grower entered into their land-sale agreement, there was no mention of the status or ownership of the tobacco crop. In early October, after the grower took possession of the property, the retiree contacted him and requested permission to harvest and remove the tobacco crop. The grower refused to allow the retiree to re-enter the property. The retiree brings suit against the grower seeking to re-enter the property and remove the tobacco crop that he had planted. Which of the following is correct regarding the respective rights of the parties?
(A) The retiree should be allowed to re-enter the property to remove the tobacco crop, but the grower is entitled to a reasonable percentage of the harvest.
(B) The retiree is entitled to remove the tobacco crop, but he must pay the grower a reasonable value to enter the property, thus gaining access to the crop.
(C) The retiree and the grower each have a colorable title to the tobacco crop, and consequently, there should be an equitable division of the proceeds from the sale of the crop between both parties.
(D) The retiree is not entitled to remove the tobacco crop and, thus, is not entitled to re-enter the property.
(E) The retiree is entitled to remove the tobacco crop, but must pay the grower for any damages caused during the harvesting process.
(F) Both the retiree and the grower are entitled to an equal share of the tobacco crop, thus, the retiree should be allowed to re-enter the property to harvest.
(G) The retiree is not entitled to remove the tobacco crop but should be compensated by the grower for the mature crop.
(H) The retiree is entitled to remove the tobacco crop and is not required to pay the grower for entering the property, thus gaining access to the crop.
(I) The grower is entitled to the tobacco crop and is not required to compensate the retiree for the mature crop.
(J) The grower is entitled to the tobacco crop, but must pay the retiree a reasonable value for the mature crop.
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
The issue is who owns unharvested annual crops (emblements) when land is conveyed after planting but before harvest. At common law, as between grantor and grantee, growing annual crops are treated as part of the realty and pass with the land at conveyance unless the seller expressly reserves them in the contract or deed. The doctrine of emblements protects tenants or holders whose estates terminate without their fault and unexpectedly; it does not apply to a voluntary sale by the owner. Here, the retiree voluntarily conveyed the farm in September, made no reservation of the tobacco crop, and now seeks to re-enter to harvest in October. Because the crops passed with the land, the buyer owns them, and the retiree has no right to remove them or to re-enter.

Final answer: (D).

(A) on the “honor system” only
(B) protected by state/provincial laws
(C) less of an issue than it is in individual therapy

(I) not applicable in group settings

target:
In group counseling, the therapist is legally and ethically bound to maintain confidentiality, but fellow group members typically are not; there is no general statute or federal mandate that legally compels members to keep others’ disclosures confidential. Therefore, members’ commitment to confidentiality is requested and reinforced by group norms and agreements but is essentially unenforceable beyond trust and ethics—i.e., the “honor system.” Options asserting legal mandates (B, D) or shifting legal responsibility to members (E, F) are inaccurate; it is not less of an issue than individual therapy (C), nor contingent on consensus or content (G, H), and it certainly applies in groups (I).

Final answer: (A).

How many ways are there to color the faces of a cube with three colors, up to rotation?
(A) 81
(B) 45
(C) 60
(D) 36
(E) 57
(F) 63
(G) 100
(H) 27
(I) 90
(J) 72
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
A: Let's think step by step. Use Burnside’s Lemma on the 24 rotations of the cube.

Rotation types and fixed colorings with 3 colors:
- Identity (1): 3^6 = 729.
- Face rotations: about 3 axes.
  - 90°/270° (6 elements): four side faces in one 4-cycle, top and bottom fixed ⇒ 3 (sides) × 3 (top) × 3 (bottom) = 27 each ⇒ 6×27 = 162.
  - 180° (3 elements): two 2-cycles among sides, top and bottom fixed ⇒ 3^4 = 81 each ⇒ 3×81 = 243.
- Vertex rotations: 120°/240° (8 elements): two 3-cycles ⇒ 3^2 = 9 each ⇒ 8×9 = 72.
- Edge rotations: 180° (6 elements): three 2-cycles ⇒ 3^3 = 27 each ⇒ 6×27 = 162.

Sum fixed colorings = 729 + 162 + 243 + 72 + 162 = 1368. Divide by 24 gives 1368/24 = 57.

Final answer: (E).

A used car worth $1650 was purchased on the installment plan with a $50 down payment and a total of $1840 in monthly payments over a period of two years. What was the rate of interest computed on the entire amount paid by the purchaser?
(A) 7.5% per year
(B) 2.5% per year
(C) 10% per year
(D) 3% per year
(E) 12% per year
(F) 5% per year
(G) 4% per year
(H) 6% per year
(I) 8.5% per year
(J) 9% per year
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
A: Let's think step by step. Cash price is $1650 with a $50 down payment, so the amount financed is $1600. The total of the installment payments over two years is $1840, so the finance charge (interest over two years) is $1840 − $1600 = $240. If interest is computed on the entire financed amount for the full term (add-on simple interest), then I = P r t, so r = 240 / (1600 × 2) = 0.075 = 7.5% per year.

Final answer: (A).

Calculate the minimum power required to cool 100 lb moles / min of air from 550°R to 500°R when the surrounding temperature is 550°R.
(A) - 1600 Btu/min or - 37.7 hp
(B) - 2100 Btu/min or - 49.8 hp
(C) - 1500 Btu/min or - 35.7 hp
(D) - 1300 Btu/min or - 30.8 hp
(E) - 2200 Btu/min or - 52.2 hp
(F) - 1900 Btu/min or - 45.0 hp
(G) - 2000 Btu/min or - 47.4 hp
(H) - 1400 Btu/min or - 33.2 hp
(I) - 1800 Btu/min or - 42.7 hp
(J) - 1700 Btu/min or - 40.7 hp
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Let’s think step by step. The minimum shaft work to cool a stream when the only heat sink is the surroundings at TH = 550 R is the reversible (Carnot) refrigeration work, integrated over the stream’s temperature range:

dWmin = dQ (TH/T − 1), with dQ = C dT and C = n·Cp.

Thus,
Wmin = ∫(from 500 to 550) C (TH/T − 1) dT = C [ TH ln(550/500) − (550 − 500) ].

Take Cp ≈ 6.6 Btu/(lbmol·R) ⇒ C = 100 × 6.6 = 660 Btu/(min·R).
Compute ln(550/500) = ln(1.1) ≈ 0.09531 ⇒ TH ln(550/500) ≈ 550 × 0.09531 = 52.42.
Bracket term: 52.42 − 50 = 2.420.
Therefore Wmin ≈ 660 × 2.420 ≈ 1.60 × 10^3 Btu/min.
Converting to hp: 1600 Btu/min ÷ 42.4 ≈ 37.7 hp.

By sign convention (work input to the refrigerator), this is −1600 Btu/min or −37.7 hp.

Final answer: (A).

White light is normally incident on a puddle of water (index of refraction 1.33). A thin (500 nm) layer of oil (index of refraction 1.5) floats on the surface of the puddle. Of the following, the most strongly reflected wavelength is
(A) 750 nm
(B) 350 nm
(C) 450 nm
(D) 650 nm
(E) 550 nm
(F) 500 nm
(G) 600 nm
(H) 400 nm
(I) 700 nm
(J) 800 nm
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
At normal incidence, the top reflection (air → oil, 1.0 → 1.5) gets a π phase shift, while the bottom reflection (oil → water, 1.5 → 1.33) gets none. Thus, reflected rays have an extra π from reflection, so constructive interference in reflection requires 2 n t = (m + 1/2) λ (λ in air). With n = 1.5 and t = 500 nm, 2 n t = 1500 nm, so λ = 1500/(m + 1/2). The visible solutions are m = 2 → λ = 600 nm and m = 3 → λ ≈ 429 nm. Among the choices, 600 nm is the exact match and thus the most strongly reflected.

Final answer: (G).

Distinguish betweenapoenzymesandcofactors .
(A) Apoenzymes can perform enzymatic functions without respective cofactors.
(B) Apoenzymes are the inactive forms of enzymes that become active upon removal of their respective cofactors.
(C) Cofactors are protein constituents and apoenzymes are non-protein portions.
(D) Apoenzymes are protein constituents that cannot perform enzymatic functions without respective cofactors, while cofactors are non-protein portions that can perform enzymatic reactions without an apoenzyme, albeit at a slower rate.
(E) Cofactors are integral to the enzyme's structure and do not participate directly in the enzymatic catalysis, whereas apoenzymes directly participate in the reaction without the need for a cofactor.
(F) Cofactors cannot perform enzymatic reactions without an apoenzyme.
(G) Cofactors are non-protein constituents that serve as temporary carriers of atoms or functional groups during enzymatic reactions, and apoenzymes are the inactive forms of enzymes.
(H) Both apoenzymes and cofactors are protein constituents that are independent of each other for enzymatic reactions.
(I) Cofactors are organic molecules that bind to the active site of an enzyme, while apoenzymes are inorganic ions that stabilize the enzyme structure.
(J) Apoenzymes are the active forms of enzymes that do not require cofactors for their activity.
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Apoenzyme is the protein portion of an enzyme and is catalytically inactive on its own; it requires a non-protein cofactor (metal ion or organic coenzyme) to form the active holoenzyme. Some cofactors, especially coenzymes, can mediate limited chemical transformations independently (far less efficiently than the holoenzyme), whereas the apoenzyme cannot function without them. Thus (D) captures the correct distinction; the other options either reverse protein/non-protein roles, claim apoenzymes work without cofactors, or misstate participation in catalysis.

Final answer: (D).

A cascade of $n$ identical independent binary symmetric channels each with raw error probability $p$, and $0<p<1$. What is the capacity of the cascade when $n$ goes to infinity?
(A) $n$
(B) 0.0
(C) 1.0
(D) $p$
(E) $\frac{1}{2}$
(F) $n(1-p)$
(G) $2p$
(H) $1-p$
(I) $p^n$
(J) $\sqrt{p}$
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
A cascade of n identical BSCs is equivalent to a single BSC with crossover probability p_n = (1 − (1 − 2p)^n)/2. For 0 < p < 1, we have |1 − 2p| < 1, so (1 − 2p)^n → 0 as n → ∞, hence p_n → 1/2. The capacity of a BSC is 1 − H2(p_n), which therefore tends to 1 − H2(1/2) = 0. Thus the cascade capacity goes to 0.

Final answer: (B).

When a contract is discharged for impossibility, any advance payments are recoverable in restitution to avoid unjust enrichment, so the woman can recover her $1,000 deposit but not expectation damages.

Eliminating choices:
- A, B, I: incorrectly deny excuse.
- C, G: incorrectly deny restitution.
- D: wrongly denies restitution.
- H: plainly incorrect.
- E labels the excuse as “supervening illegality,” which is treated under the impossibility/impracticability doctrine; the best-stated answer tracks impossibility and restitution.

(F) III only

II: With air resistance present, at the top the instantaneous velocity is zero, so the drag force is zero; at that instant the ball is momentarily at rest and its instantaneous acceleration is zero. True. 
III: Drag adds to gravity on the way up (slowing it more) and opposes gravity on the way down (slowing the fall), so the ascent is quicker and the descent slower; it is not longer going up than coming down. False.

The dean responded, "I'm sorry, but there is absolutely nothing I can do about it. "The applicant asserted a claim against the law school for breach of contract. The applicant offers to introduce evidence that during his telephone conversation with dean of faculty, the dean promised, if necessary, to provide him with additional sick days. The most accurate statement concerning the dean's oral promise would be that
(A) parol evidence is admissible to show that the parties assented to their written contract only as a partial integration of their complete contract.
(B) parol evidence is admissible to prove the written contract was a mistake.
(C) parol evidence is admissible to show the dean's personal guarantee of additional sick days.
(D) parol evidence is admissible to show that the written contract was void due to lack of consideration.
(E) parol evidence is inadmissible to prove any oral agreements made prior to the signing of the written contract.
(F) parol evidence is admissible to show that the applicant misunderstood the terms of the contract.
(G) parol evidence is admissible to prove the dean coerced the applicant into signing the contract.
(H) parol evidence is admissible to show that the written contract is not enforceable, because of undue influence or fraud.
(I) parol evidence is inadmissible to prove contemporaneous oral agreements that vary or contradict the terms of a written contract.
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I).
target:
A: Let's think step by step. The parties ended with a detailed written employment contract expressly providing five paid sick days. The applicant seeks to introduce evidence of the dean’s earlier oral assurance of additional sick days. Under the parol evidence rule, prior or contemporaneous oral agreements that vary or contradict the terms of an integrated writing are inadmissible; exceptions (e.g., fraud, mistake, duress, ambiguity, or a consistent collateral agreement) do not apply here. The promised extra sick leave directly contradicts the written five-day limit and was made before the applicant signed, so it is barred. Option E is overbroad (there are exceptions), whereas Option I precisely states the applicable rule. Therefore, the most accurate statement is that such parol evidence is inadmissible.

Final answer: (I).

(A) 4 in
(B) 4.5 in
(C) 2.5 in
(D) 5 in
(E) 7 in
(F) 6 in
(G) 3.5 in
(H) 3 in
(I) 6.5 in
(J) 5.5 in

target:
Let's think step by step. Treat the bar as a linear spring with stiffness k = AE/L. A weight W dropped from height h onto the bar gives maximum load Pmax satisfying energy: W(h + x) = (1/2)k x^2, so Pmax = kx = W + sqrt(W^2 + 2Whk). Here W = 550 lb, h = 4 in, L = 14 in, E ≈ 30×10^6 psi, k = A E / L.

Compute 2Whk = 2(550)(4)(A·30×10^6/14) ≈ 9.4286×10^9 A, so
Pmax = 550 + sqrt(550^2 + 9.4286×10^9 A).

Set allowable stress σ = Pmax/A = 22,000 psi:
22,000 A = 550 + sqrt(550^2 + 9.4286×10^9 A).

Solving gives A ≈ 19.53 in^2. Then d = sqrt(4A/π) ≈ sqrt(78.12/3.1416) ≈ 4.99 in, i.e., about 5 in.

Mrs. Walter gave an exam in a mathematics class of five students. She entered the scores in random order into a spreadsheet, which recalculated the class average after each score was entered. Mrs. Walter noticed that after each score was entered, the average was always an integer. The scores (listed in ascending order) were 71,76,80,82,and 91. What was the last score Mrs. Walter entered?
(A) 82
(B) 91
(C) 71
(D) The score that was the mode of the five scores
(E) The score that made the average 75
(F) 80
(G) A score not listed among the five provided scores

(J) The score that made the average 79

target:
A: Let’s think step by step. The total of all five scores is 400. If the last score is x, then the sum of the first four is 400 − x and must be divisible by 4, so x ≡ 0 (mod 4). Among the given scores, only 76 and 80 fit. If x = 76, the first four are {71, 80, 82, 91}. For the third average to be an integer, the sum of the first three must be divisible by 3. Mod 3 residues are 71≡2, 80≡2, 82≡1, 91≡1; no triple among these sums to 0 mod 3 (you’d need three 1’s or three 2’s), so this is impossible. Therefore x must be 80. Indeed, an order such as 76, 82, 91, 71, 80 yields running averages 76, 79, 83, 80, 80, all integers.
Final answer: (F).

Can chromosome instability cause cancer ?
(A) Chromosomal instability prevents cancer.
(B) Chromosomal instability is solely caused by the presence of cancer.
(C) Chromosomal instability has no link to cancer.
(D) Chromosomal instability is only found in benign tumors and not associated with cancerous growths.
(E) Chromosomal instability can be reversed, thereby curing cancer.
(F) Chromosomal instability protects cells from becoming cancerous by enhancing genetic diversity.
(G) Chromosomal instability directly causes cancer.
(H) Chromosomal instability is a type of cancer.
(I) Chromosomal instability is the result of a healthy immune response to cancer cells.

Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
A: Let's think step by step. Chromosomal instability (CIN) leads to ongoing gains/losses of chromosomes and structural rearrangements, increasing aneuploidy and mutational diversity. This can accelerate the emergence and selection of oncogenic clones and loss of tumor suppressors, creating a permissive environment for tumor initiation and progression. However, CIN is not itself a cancer (reject H), not solely an effect of existing cancer (reject B), and not uniformly protective (reject A, F). It doesn’t have no link (reject C), isn’t limited to benign tumors (reject D), and there’s no general cure by reversing CIN (reject E). Saying it “directly causes cancer” (G) overstates causality; CIN is a facilitating factor that increases susceptibility. Thus, it may create an environment highly susceptible to certain cancers.

Final answer: (J).

Her brother and maternal uncle had Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) and died at the ages of 28 and 17 years, respectively. Genetic analysis was not performed on either relative prior to death. Which of the following is the most appropriate assessment of this patient's carrier status for this disease?

(B) The patient's mother is a carrier, hence the patient is definitely a carrier too
(C) The patient is a carrier of the disease based on her family history of DMD
(D) The patient's DMD carrier status is uncertain due to lack of genetic analysis on her deceased relatives.
(E) The patient has a 25% risk of having a child with DMD
(F) The patient is a carrier of the disease based on her mother's elevated creatine kinase concentration
(G) The patient's DMD carrier status is certain due to maternal transmission

target:
Duchenne muscular dystrophy is X-linked recessive. The pedigree (affected maternal uncle and affected brother) makes the patient’s mother very likely a carrier, and her elevated CK supports this, but neither elevated CK in the mother nor normal CK in the patient can confirm or exclude carrier status (CK is neither sensitive nor specific for carrier detection; many carriers have normal CK). The patient’s normal CK therefore does not rule out carrier status, and family history alone cannot make her definitively a carrier. Precise carrier assessment is best done by identifying the pathogenic DMD variant in an affected male and then testing at-risk females; because the affected relatives are deceased and no familial mutation is known, the patient’s carrier status remains uncertain at this time. Options claiming certainty (A, B, C, F, G, J) are incorrect; risk estimates for offspring (E, H) require confirmed carrier status; random X inactivation (I) affects phenotype/CK but not the ability to determine genotype if the familial variant were known.

The friend consented, and the employee showed him how to use the control switch. When the vacationer approached the top, the employee was still away. Instead of stopping the lift to permit the vacationer to get off, the friend allowed the lift to keep moving. The vacationer was carried past the platform, and he was swung violently from side to side as the ski lift started downward. When the employee returned and sized up the situation, he threw the switch, stopping the lift. The vacationer, severely bruised and badly frightened, jumped off the ski lift and completed his descent by foot. In a personal injury action by the vacationer against the owner of the private park, the vacationer will rely on the concept of
(A) vicarious liability.

(C) imputed negligence.

(F) joint venture.
(G) strict liability.
(H) premises liability.
(I) respondeat superior.
(J) contributory negligence.

target:
A: The injury resulted from the negligent operation of the ski lift by the ski lift company’s stand-in “friend,” not from a static dangerous condition of the land. The park owner is not the employer of the lift operator, so respondeat superior/vicarious liability against the owner does not directly apply. To reach the owner, the vacationer needs to impute the lift operator’s negligence to the owner. The facts show a concession agreement with profit sharing (15% of net proceeds) and a common business purpose in running the lift for paying patrons—classic indicators of a joint enterprise/joint venture, under which each co-venturer is vicariously liable for torts committed in furtherance of the venture. The other options are inapposite: negligence per se lacks a statutory violation; strict liability doesn’t fit; contributory/comparative negligence and assumption of risk are defenses, not the theory of liability; premises liability targets property conditions, not negligent lift operation by a concessionaire. Therefore, the plaintiff will rely on joint venture to hold the owner liable.

Final answer: (F).

The standard E° for(1/2)F_2(g) + e^- \rightarrow F^-(aq), which is +2.87V, applies when the flouride ion concentration is 1M . What would the corresponding E be in 1M H_3O^+ , i.e., for the electrode reaction (1/2)F_2(g) + e^- + H_3O^+ \rightarrow HF(aq) +H_2O? (The K_diss of HF is 6.7 × 10^-4).
(A) +3.06V
(B) +3.16V
(C) +3.26V
(D) +2.77V
(E) +2.87V
(F) +2.96V
(G) +2.67V
(H) +2.57V
(I) +3.36V
(J) +3.45V
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
The HF-coupled half-reaction is obtained by adding the chemical equilibrium F− + H3O+ → HF + H2O to (1/2)F2 + e− → F−. The equilibrium constant for F− + H3O+ → HF + H2O is K = 1/Ka = 1/(6.7×10^-4) ≈ 1.49×10^3. Adding equilibria adds standard free energies, so the potential shifts by ΔE = (RT/F) ln K. At 298 K, ΔE ≈ 0.0257 ln(1.49×10^3) ≈ 0.0257×7.31 ≈ 0.188 V. Therefore, E ≈ 2.87 V + 0.19 V ≈ 3.06 V.
Final answer: (A).

Which of the following sets forth the strongest constitutional argument the candidate could make against the interpretation of the statute by the state officials?
(A) It unreasonably discriminates against German voters who lack a proficiency in the English language.
(B) It unreasonably interferes with the exclusive federal election power as embodied in the Fifteenth Amendment.
(C) It goes against the spirit of the Twenty-fourth Amendment by effectively imposing a "literacy tax."
(D) It unreasonably discriminates against non-English speaking voters in general.
(E) It violates the Equal Protection Clause by treating write-in candidates differently.
(F) It unreasonably discriminates against write-in candidates for public office.
(G) It unreasonably limits the means by which a candidate can campaign, infringing on democratic practices.
(H) It unreasonably restricts freedom of speech as guaranteed by the First Amendment.
(I) It unreasonably interferes with the 1965 Voting Rights Act outlawing literacy tests.
(J) It interferes with the right to vote as guaranteed by the Nineteenth Amendment.
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
A: Let's think step by step. States can regulate ballot mechanics, but when a rule uniquely burdens write-in candidacies and their supporters, it implicates the First and Fourteenth Amendment voting and associational rights under the Anderson-Burdick framework. Requiring that write-ins be hand-printed in ink, while disallowing accurate stickers that clearly identify the candidate and office, places a special, nontrivial burden on write-in candidates and their voters that does not apply to candidates already printed on the ballot. That is the strongest constitutional angle.

A and D are weak because the rule is facially neutral and there’s no showing of intentional discrimination based on national origin or language (Washington v. Davis). B and J invoke the wrong amendments. C (poll tax) is inapposite. I invokes a statute (VRA), not the Constitution, and the measure is not a classic literacy test. H and G miscast the issue as general speech or campaign-method restrictions; ballot-format rules in a nonpublic forum get more leeway. E’s pure Equal Protection framing is weaker than the established First/Fourteenth ballot-access analysis that most directly targets discrimination against write-in candidacies. Thus, the best argument is that the interpretation unreasonably discriminates against write-in candidates. 

Final answer: (F).

suppose sequence x_n satisfies x_n*x_{n+1}=n for all n>=1, and $\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}=1$. What's the value of $\pi*x_1^2$?
(A) 3.14
(B) 2.0
(C) 4.0
(D) 2.5
(E) 6.0
(F) 5.0
(G) 0.5
(H) 1.0

(J) 3.0

A: Let's think step by step. From x_n x_{n+1} = n, we get x_{n+2} = x_n (n+1)/n, hence
- x_{2k-1} = x_1 ∏_{j=1}^{k-1} (2j)/(2j−1), so x_{2k-1}^2/(2k−1) = x_1^2 ∏_{j=1}^{k-1} (2j)^2/(2j−1)^2 · 1/(2k−1).
Using ∏_{j=1}^{k-1} (2j+1)/(2j−1) = 2k−1, this becomes x_1^2 ∏_{j=1}^{k-1} (2j)^2/[(2j−1)(2j+1)].
By Wallis’ product, this tends to x_1^2 · (π/2). The given limit x_n/x_{n+1} → 1 implies x_{2k-1}^2/(2k−1) → 1, so x_1^2 · (π/2) = 1 ⇒ x_1^2 = 2/π.
Therefore π x_1^2 = 2.

Final answer: (B).

Which of the following statements are true concerning the Box-Jenkins approach to diagnostic testing for ARMA models?

(i) The tests will show whether the identified model is either too large or too small

(ii) The tests involve checking the model residuals for autocorrelation, heteroscedasticity, and non-normality

(iii) If the model suggested at the identification stage is appropriate, the acf and pacf for the residuals should show no additional structure

(iv) If the model suggested at the identification stage is appropriate, the coefficients on the additional variables under the overfitting approach will be statistically insignificant
(A) (iii) and (iv) only
(B) (i) and (iii) only
(C) (i), (iii), and (iv) only
(D) (i), (ii), and (iii) only
(E) (ii) and (iii) only
(F) (i) and (iv) only
(G) (i), (ii), (iii), and (iv)
(H) (ii) and (iv) only
(I) (ii), (iii), and (iv) only
(J) (i) and (ii) only
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Let's think step by step.

(i) False. Residual-based diagnostics primarily detect underfitting (model too small) via remaining autocorrelation. They do not reliably indicate overfitting (too large); parsimony is judged by significance and information criteria, not residual diagnostics.

(ii) True. Box–Jenkins diagnostics include checking residuals for autocorrelation (ACF/Ljung–Box), and, under the Gaussian white-noise assumption, also checking for non-normality and heteroscedasticity (e.g., ARCH effects).

(iii) False as stated. For an adequate model, residuals should be indistinguishable from white noise in a joint sense (e.g., pass Ljung–Box). Demanding that the residual ACF and PACF show “no additional structure” at every lag is too strong; small random spikes can occur, and PACF of residuals is not central to the diagnostics.

(iv) True. Under the overfitting approach, adding higher-order AR/MA terms should yield insignificant coefficients if the identified model is appropriate.

Therefore, the true statements are (ii) and (iv) only.

Final answer: (H).

Statement 1 | Every homomorphic image of a group G is isomorphic to a factor group of G. Statement 2 | The homomorphic images of a group G are the same (up to isomorphism) as the factor groups of G.

(C) False, the statements cannot be evaluated

(J) True, False

target:
By the First Isomorphism Theorem, for any homomorphism φ: G → H, the image φ(G) ≅ G/ker φ, so every homomorphic image is isomorphic to a factor group of G (Statement 1 true). Conversely, for any normal subgroup N ⊲ G, the natural projection G → G/N is a homomorphism whose image is G/N, so every factor group arises as a homomorphic image (up to isomorphism), making Statement 2 true.

Final answer: (A).

Let {X_n: n \geq 1} be independent, identically distributed random variables taking integer values {1,-1}. Let S_0=0, S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n X_i. Let P(X_i=1)=0.8 and P(X_i=-1)=0.2. The range R_n of S_0,S_1,...,S_n is the number of distinct values taken by the sequence. Then what is the limit of n^{-1}E[R_n] as n goes to infinity? Here E[R_n] is the expectation over the random variable R_n.

(C) 1.2
(D) 1.6
(E) 0.8
(F) 0.9
(G) 1.4

Here the drift is positive: μ = E[X_1] = 0.8 − 0.2 = 0.6. Then S_n/n → μ a.s., and with positive drift the number of new minima is finite a.s., so m_n is a finite random variable and m_n/n → 0. Also M_n/n → μ a.s. (since M_n − S_n is sublinear). Hence R_n/n → μ, and therefore n^{-1} E[R_n] → μ = 0.6.

(B) 1.036 in.
(C) 2.036 in.
(D) 1.336 in.
(E) 0.936 in.

We’re sizing a circular compression member against Euler buckling with a factor of safety. Assume pin-ended column (K = 1). Required: P_cr ≥ FS × P = 3 × 90,000 = 270,000 lb.

Length L = 0.95 ft = 11.4 in. For a solid circular bar, I = (π/64) d^4. Euler buckling:

Solve for d:
d = [ (P_cr · 64 · L^2) / (π^3 E) ]^(1/4)
= [ (270,000 · 64 · 11.4^2) / (π^3 · 31×10^6) ]^(1/4)
≈ [ 2.2457×10^9 / 9.61195×10^8 ]^(1/4) ≈ (2.336)^(1/4) ≈ 1.236 in.

What power was granted to the states by the Articles of Confederation but not by the Constitution?
(A) Enforcing national laws
(B) Authorizing constitutional amendments
(C) Establishing foreign diplomatic relations
(D) Coining money
(E) Having power to declare war
(F) Appealing to the president to adjudicate disputes

target:
A: Let's think step by step. Under the Articles of Confederation, states retained broad sovereignty, including the authority to issue their own currency—multiple state coinages and bills of credit circulated. The Constitution then expressly prohibits states from coining money (Article I, Section 10), reserving monetary powers to the federal government. The other options are either federal powers under both systems (declaring war, foreign diplomacy), shared or still present in some form under the Constitution (taxing, representation, amendment ratification), or inapplicable (no president under the Articles). Hence, the unique state power removed by the Constitution is coining money.

Final answer: (D).

(A) $6,000,000 reduction

(D) $3,000,000 increase

target:

With a required reserve ratio of 25%, the simple money multiplier is 1/0.25 = 4. Since banks are fully loaned up (no excess reserves) and assuming no currency drain, the contraction in the money supply equals the change in reserves times the multiplier: 3,000,000 × 4 = 12,000,000. Therefore, the money supply falls by $12,000,000. 

Final answer: (B).

Distilled water is a dielectric having the constants\epsilon_r= 81, \mu_r= 1. If a wave is incident from water onto a water-air interface, calculate the critical angle. If the incident E_1 = 1 V m^-1 and the incident angle is 45°, calculate the magnitude of the field strength in the air (a) at the interface and (b) \lambda/4 away from the interface.
(A) at the interface: 2.42 Vm^-1, away from the interface: 83.2 \mu Vm^-1
(B) at the interface: 1.00 Vm^-1, away from the interface: 50.0 \mu Vm^-1
(C) at the interface: 1.42 Vm^-1, away from the interface: 73.2 \mu Vm^-1
(D) at the interface: 0.92 Vm^-1, away from the interface: 30.4 \mu Vm^-1
(E) at the interface: 1.22 Vm^-1, away from the interface: 60.5 \mu Vm^-1
(F) at the interface: 0.72 Vm^-1, away from the interface: 37.2 \mu Vm^-1
(G) at the interface: 1.62 Vm^-1, away from the interface: 40.8 \mu Vm^-1
(H) at the interface: 1.82 Vm^-1, away from the interface: 63.2 \mu Vm^-1
(I) at the interface: 2.00 Vm^-1, away from the interface: 90.0 \mu Vm^-1
(J) at the interface: 1.52 Vm^-1, away from the interface: 80.2 \mu Vm^-1
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H/I/J).
target:
Let’s think step by step.

- Refractive indices: n1 = √(εrμr) = √(81·1) = 9 (water), n2 ≈ 1 (air).
- Critical angle for water→air: sin θc = n2/n1 = 1/9 ⇒ θc ≈ arcsin(1/9) ≈ 6.38°. Since θi = 45° > θc, transmission is evanescent in air.

At the interface (assume TE/s-polarization):
- For TIR, cos θ2 = j√((n1 sin θ1/n2)^2 − 1) = j√((9·sin45°)^2 − 1) = j√(40.5 − 1) = j·6.283.
- Fresnel transmission coefficient (TE) for the E-field:
  t_s = 2 n1 cos θ1 / (n1 cos θ1 + n2 cos θ2).
  With n1 cos θ1 = 9·0.7071 = 6.364, numerator = 12.728, denominator = 6.364 + j6.283.
  |t_s| = 12.728 / √(6.364^2 + 6.283^2) ≈ 12.728 / 8.944 ≈ 1.423.
- Thus |E_t(0)| = |t_s|·E_i ≈ 1.423·1 V/m ≈ 1.42 V/m.

At a distance λ/4 into air:
- Evanescent decay constant α = k0 √((n1 sin θ1)^2 − n2^2) = k0·6.283, with k0 = 2π/λ0 and λ (in air) = λ0.
- Over z = λ/4 = λ0/4: αz = (2π/λ0)(λ0/4)·6.283 = (π/2)·6.283 ≈ 9.87.
- Field magnitude: |E_t(z)| = |E_t(0)| e^{-αz} ≈ 1.423·e^{-9.87} ≈ 1.423·5.2×10^-5 ≈ 7.36×10^-5 V/m ≈ 73.2 μV/m.

Final answer: (C).

In what ways do theories of conventional and critical social constructivism differ?
(A) Critical constructivism argues that conventional constructivism is too focused on rationalist approaches and fails to account for the role of power and inequality in social constructions.
(B) Conventional constructivism refers to the earlier works of social constructivist theorists that are characterised by a rejection of positivist methodology. Critical constructivists challenge these theories, embracing positivist methodologies in their work.
(C) Conventional constructivists view constructivism as a bridge between rationalist and reflectivist approaches, enabling both to benefit from the insights of the other. Conversely, critical constructivists argue that this approach simply reproduces the binary distinctions that characterize positivist methodology.
(D) Conventional constructivism views social reality as a product of individual cognitive processes, while critical constructivism views it as a product of societal and institutional structures.
(E) Conventional constructivism argues for a positivist methodology while critical constructivism rejects this in favor of a more interpretivist approach.
(F) Critical constructivism regards the strength of social constructivism to be its ability to bridge rationalist and reflectivity approaches. Conversely, social constructivists regard this as an unhelpful regression to positivist binary frameworks of analysis.
(G) Social constructivism is best understood as a uniform approach to the construction of social identities and security; although distinctions exist within the field, these are not easily defined as distinctive separate approaches.
(H) Conventional constructivism and critical constructivism are two different names for the same approach in social theory, with no significant differences between them.
Answer with a single letter (A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H).
target:
Conventional constructivists (e.g., Wendt, Adler) position constructivism as a bridge to mainstream rationalism, employing quasi-positivist or explanatory methods while emphasizing the causal/constitutive role of norms and identities. Critical constructivists, drawing on critical theory and post-structuralism, reject this bridge-building as reproducing positivist binaries (e.g., structure/agency, material/ideational) and masking power, discourse, and inequality. This directly matches option (C). Options (B), (E), and (F) invert or oversimplify the methodological stances; (A) is partially true but lacks the key “bridge” critique; (D), (G), and (H) mischaracterize or erase distinctions.

Final answer: (C).

(B) 9
(C) 1
(D) 8
(E) 4
(F) 2

(H) 10
(I) 7
(J) 5

Let z = a + bi with a, b ∈ Z_{>0}. Then
